w:r.w:t: Student Portal · Admin Console · CSR Partner Approval · Fund Disbursement
9. Proposed Screens for UI / Figma Design

This document defines the business and functional requirements for a Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) Student Scholarship Management System. The platform enables eligible students to discover scholarship programmes, maintain their profile and supporting evidence, and apply for funding. It enables programme administrators to evaluate applicants through automated matching, document audit, and background verification, secure CSR partner approval, and disburse funds to students or to their schools and colleges.

The system covers the end-to-end scholarship lifecycle across two primary experiences — a Student Portal and an Admin Console — together with a CSR Partner approval step and a Finance disbursement step.
Student self-registration, profile management, personal and family economic details, and document upload.
Catalogue of scholarship programmes with eligibility criteria and application windows.
Eligibility-based auto-matching of student profiles to scholarship programmes.
Application submission, tracking, and status notifications.
Admin review queue: auto-matched profiles, document audit, background-check capture, and screening decisions.
CSR partner review and acceptance of shortlisted students.
Fund disbursement to students or to institutions, with reconciliation and receipts.
Role-based access control, audit logging, notifications, and reporting.
Academic performance tracking after disbursement (future phase / renewals module).
Direct integration with national identity verification beyond document upload (assumed manual/third-party in phase 1).
Mobile native apps — the phase-1 delivery targets a responsive web application.
CSR programme sponsors and steering committee.
Programme management office and scholarship administrators.
CSR partner representatives and finance/disbursement team.
Product, design, and engineering delivery teams.
Corporate Social Responsibility — corporate-funded social initiatives, here funding student scholarships.
Unique identifier assigned to each registered student in the system.
Rules-based engine that maps a student profile to scholarships whose eligibility criteria are met.
Admin verification that uploaded documents are authentic, legible, and match declared details.
Verification of declared family economic and personal details by an authorised officer.
Admin decision step to accept or reject a candidate for onward CSR partner review.
Funding organisation that gives final acceptance and commits funds to selected students.
Transfer of scholarship funds to the student or to the institution (school/college).
Service Level Agreement — target turnaround for a process step.

The Scholarship Management System digitises a previously manual, paper-heavy scholarship process. It reduces administrative effort, improves transparency for students, strengthens fairness through consistent eligibility rules, and gives CSR partners auditable evidence that funds reach genuinely eligible beneficiaries.
Make scholarship discovery and application self-service for students.
Majority of applications submitted online without staff assistance.
Increase fairness and consistency in shortlisting.
Eligibility applied uniformly via auto-match rules.
Reduce time from application to disbursement.
Shorter average cycle time vs. the manual baseline.
Provide CSR partners with auditable selection evidence.
Ensure funds reach verified, eligible beneficiaries.
Disbursement only after multi-step verification and partner approval.
Transparency — students always see where their application stands.
Auditability — every decision is logged with who, what, and when.
Data privacy — sensitive personal and economic data is access-controlled and encrypted.
Configurability — eligibility criteria and scholarship rules are configurable, not hard-coded.
Separation of duties — review, screening, partner approval, and disbursement are distinct roles.

The system supports the following roles. Permissions are enforced through RBAC; a single person may hold more than one role subject to separation-of-duties rules (e.g., the disbursement approver should not also be the screening officer for the same case).
Register, maintain profile, upload documents, browse and apply for scholarships, track status.
Create/edit own profile; submit applications; view own status.
Configure scholarships and eligibility; manage review queues; take screening decisions.
Configure programmes; view matched profiles; screen accept/reject.
Audit uploaded documents for authenticity, legibility, and consistency.
View documents; mark verified/rejected; request re-upload.
Verify declared family economic and personal details; record results.
Review shortlisted candidates; accept or decline for funding.
View shortlist & evidence; record partner acceptance.
Disburse funds to students or institutions; reconcile and issue receipts.
Initiate/approve disbursement; upload proof; mark completed.
Manage users, roles, configuration, and audit logs.
Full administrative configuration; no access to alter audit history.
A student can self-register using email and/or mobile number with OTP verification.
The system enforces a secure password policy and supports password reset.
On first login the student is guided through a profile-completion wizard.
A profile completeness indicator shows percentage complete and missing items.
The student can capture personal details: full name, date of birth, gender, category, contact, and address.
The student can capture academic details: current institution, course, year, marks/GPA, and prior qualifications.
The student can edit and save the profile in stages; partial saves are retained.
The system validates field formats (dates, phone, email, numeric marks) before save.
The student can declare household income, number of dependents, parents'/guardians' occupation, and income sources.
The student can declare special circumstances (single parent, disability, first-generation learner, etc.).
Declared economic details feed the eligibility auto-match and the background-check step.
Sensitive economic fields are masked in lists and visible only to authorised roles.
The student can upload required documents (ID proof, income certificate, marksheets, bank/institution details, photo).
The system accepts common formats (PDF, JPG, PNG) with size limits and shows upload status.
Each scholarship can specify its own required-document checklist; the portal shows what is pending.
If a reviewer rejects a document, the student is notified and can re-upload.
The student can browse and search the catalogue of active scholarships with eligibility and deadlines.
The system highlights scholarships the student is auto-matched to, based on profile and economic data.
The student can view full scholarship details, benefits, criteria, and required documents.
The student can apply for a desired scholarship; the system blocks applications that fail mandatory eligibility.
The student can track each application's status through every stage with timestamps.
The student receives notifications (email/SMS/in-app) at key status changes.
An admin can create and configure scholarship programmes: name, sponsor/CSR partner, benefit amount, slots, window, and status.
An admin can define eligibility criteria (income ceiling, category, course, marks threshold, location, special circumstances).
An admin can configure the required-document checklist per scholarship.
Eligibility rules are configurable without code changes and versioned on change.
The system auto-matches student profiles to scholarships using configured eligibility rules and presents a match score/flags.
An admin can view a queue of auto-matched applicants per scholarship, with filters and sorting.
An admin can open a consolidated applicant view: personal, academic, economic, documents, and match rationale.
An admin can override a match with a recorded reason (exception handling).
A reviewer can view each uploaded document and mark it Verified, Rejected, or Needs Re-upload, with notes.
The system tracks audit status per document and aggregates a document-verification status per application.
Rejecting a document triggers a notification to the student and reopens that checklist item.
All document-audit actions are recorded in the audit log with reviewer identity and timestamp.
A verification officer can record background-check results for declared economic/personal details: Pass, Fail, or Inconclusive.
The officer can attach supporting evidence and free-text findings.
The background-check outcome is visible to screening and partner-review steps but restricted from the student.
Background-check status updates are captured in the audit trail.
An admin can screen an applicant and decide Accept or Reject, only after document audit and background check are complete.
Accepting an applicant makes the student a candidate in the programme and forwards the case to the CSR partner.
Rejection requires a reason; the student is notified with appropriate messaging.
The screening screen consolidates match rationale, document status, and background-check result in one view.
A CSR partner representative can view the screened shortlist with supporting evidence for their funded programme(s).
The partner can Accept or Decline each candidate; acceptance commits the candidate for disbursement.
Partner decisions are recorded with identity, timestamp, and optional remarks.
Slot limits are enforced; the system prevents partner acceptances beyond available slots/budget.
A finance officer can initiate disbursement for partner-accepted candidates to the student or the institution.
The system captures beneficiary bank/institution details and disbursement amount, with maker-checker approval.
The officer can record disbursement status (Initiated, Completed, Failed) and upload proof/receipt.
On completion the student is notified and the application is marked Funded; reconciliation reports are available.
The scholarship lifecycle moves through clearly separated stages with defined ownership. The accompanying workflow diagram (provided separately as a draw.io-importable file) visualises the same flow.
Eligibility fail at auto-match: application flagged ineligible; student notified with reason.
Document rejected at audit: checklist item reopened; student re-uploads; audit resumes.
Background check fail: case routed back to screening for reject decision with mandatory reason.
Screening reject: student notified; case closed (with optional appeal path in a future phase).
Partner decline: case returned to admin; candidate may be re-pooled for an alternate scholarship.
Disbursement failure: finance officer retries or corrects beneficiary details; status stays Initiated until resolved.
The following entities form the logical data model. Attributes listed are indicative and will be finalised during detailed design.
Applicant ID, name, DOB, gender, category, contact, address, academic details.
Household income, dependents, occupation, income sources, special circumstances.
Type, file reference, audit status, reviewer, timestamp.
Name, CSR partner, benefit, slots, window, eligibility ruleset, document checklist.
Student, scholarship, status, match score, timestamps.
Beneficiary type, amount, status, proof, approver, timestamps.
RBAC for every screen and action; encryption of sensitive data at rest and in transit; session timeout.
Personal and economic data access restricted by role; data-minimisation in list views; consent capture at registration.
Immutable audit log of all decisions and document actions, exportable for partner assurance.
Common screens respond quickly under expected concurrent load; large document uploads handled asynchronously.
Targeted high availability during application windows; graceful degradation and clear error messaging.
Support growth in students, scholarships, and partners without redesign.
No fund disbursement without completed verification and partner acceptance; maker-checker on financial actions.
Configurable eligibility and document rules; clear separation of configuration from code.
Aligned to applicable data-protection norms; retention and deletion policies for applicant data.
Funnel report: registrations → applications → screened → partner-accepted → funded.
SLA/turnaround report per stage and per reviewer.
Disbursement and reconciliation report by scholarship and CSR partner.
Diversity/coverage report by category, region, and economic band (privacy-aggregated).
Audit export per beneficiary for CSR partner assurance.
9. Proposed Screens for UI / Figma Design
The following screens are recommended as a starting set for high-fidelity Figma design. They are grouped by experience and ordered to mirror the workflow.
Email-mobile + OTP, password, social proof, programme banner.
Stepper, personal & academic fields, completeness meter.
Income, dependents, occupation, special circumstances; privacy note.
Per-scholarship checklist, drag-drop, status chips, re-upload prompts.
Search/filter, eligibility tags, 'Matched for you' highlight.
Benefits, criteria, required documents, Apply CTA.
Pre-filled review, consent, submit, success state.
Timeline per stage, notifications, action items.
Profile completeness, matched scholarships, alerts, deadlines.
Queue counts, SLA alerts, funnel snapshot, disbursement summary.
Create/edit programme, eligibility rule builder, document checklist.
List with match score, filters, bulk actions.
Personal/economic/academic + match rationale in one view.
Document viewer, verify/reject, notes, re-upload request.
Shortlist, evidence pack, Accept/Decline, slot tracker.
Beneficiary details, amount, maker-checker, proof upload, status.
Funnel, SLA, disbursement, per-beneficiary audit export.
End-to-end lifecycle swimlane (Student → System → Admin → CSR Partner → Finance) — the primary flow in the attached draw.io file.
Document audit & re-upload loop (sub-flow) showing reviewer decisions and student re-upload.
Screening decision flow showing dependencies on audit and background check.
Disbursement maker-checker flow including failure/retry handling.

A companion workflow file (WORKFLOW_scholarship_drawio.md) accompanies this document. It contains a Mermaid flowchart that can be imported directly into draw.io (diagrams.net) via Arrange → Insert → Mermaid, or Extras → Edit Diagram, to generate an editable diagram.
Students have access to a smartphone or computer and a valid email or mobile number.
CSR partners will nominate representatives to perform acceptance within agreed SLAs.
Required document types and eligibility norms are provided by the programme office before launch.
Disbursement is performed via an existing finance/banking process integrated or operated alongside the system.
Phase-1 delivery is a responsive web application; native mobile apps are deferred.
Identity and income verification rely on uploaded documents plus officer review in phase 1.
Fund movement is subject to the organisation's financial controls and approval hierarchy.
Mandatory document audit, background check, and audit trail.
RBAC, encryption, data masking, least-privilege access.
End of document. Targets, SLAs, and detailed field lists are indicative and to be finalised with the programme office and CSR partners during detailed design.
